approaches.name: Brute Force O(log n) Iterative Division
approaches.summary: Repeatedly divide the decimal number by 2, collecting remainders in reverse order to build the binary representation. This is the most straightforward method, suitable for beginners learning number conversion.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Convert Decimal to Binary Using Iterative Division
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of decimal-to-binary conversion using repeated division by 2.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires converting a non-negative integer from decimal (base-10) to binary (base-2) representation. The iterative division approach works by repeatedly dividing the number by 2 and collecting remainders. The intuition is that binary representation expresses a number as a sum of powers of 2, and division by 2 isolates the least significant bit (remainder) while reducing the number for the next iteration. The algorithm: 1) Handle edge case of 0 (binary '0'). 2) Initialize an empty list for remainders. 3) While the number is greater than 0: a) Compute remainder = number % 2. b) Append remainder to list. c) Update number = number // 2 (integer division). 4) Reverse the list of remainders. 5) Convert to string. Time complexity is O(log n) because the number of divisions equals the number of bits in binary representation (⌊log₂n⌋ + 1). Space complexity is O(log n) for storing remainders. Connection to L1: This uses the remainder collection subroutine as its core component. Common mistakes: forgetting to handle 0 separately, not reversing remainders (binary reads from last remainder to first), or incorrect integer division. Real code pattern: def decimal_to_binary(n): if n == 0: return '0'; binary_digits = []; while n > 0: binary_digits.append(str(n % 2)); n //= 2; return ''.join(reversed(binary_digits)).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Collect Remainders via Division by 2
ladders.desc: Implement the core loop that divides a number by 2 and collects remainders until it reaches 0.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the conversion algorithm. The intuition: repeatedly dividing by 2 extracts the binary digits from least significant to most significant. Each division yields a remainder (0 or 1), which represents the current binary digit, and reduces the number for the next iteration. Algorithm: 1) Input a positive integer n. 2) Initialize an empty list for remainders. 3) While n > 0: a) remainder = n % 2. b) Append remainder to list. c) n = n // 2 (integer division). 4) Return the list of remainders. Time: O(log n) for the loop iterations. Space: O(log n) for the list. Connection to L0: This subroutine provides the digit sequence that L0 reverses and formats. Common mistakes: infinite loop if n is not updated (e.g., forgetting n = n // 2), or incorrect order (remainders are collected LSB-first). Real code pattern: def collect_remainders(n): remainders = []; while n > 0: remainders.append(n % 2); n //= 2; return remainders.

examples.trace: Step 1: n=6 > 0. remainder=6%2=0, remainders=[0], n=6//2=3. Step 2: n=3 > 0. remainder=3%2=1, remainders=[0,1], n=3//2=1. Step 3: n=1 > 0. remainder=1%2=1, remainders=[0,1,1], n=1//2=0. Step 4: n=0, stop. Return [0,1,1] (note: LSB first, so binary is 110 when reversed).

Step 1: n=9 > 0. remainder=9%2=1, remainders=[1], n=9//2=4. Step 2: n=4 > 0. remainder=4%2=0, remainders=[1,0], n=4//2=2. Step 3: n=2 > 0. remainder=2%2=0, remainders=[1,0,0], n=2//2=1. Step 4: n=1 > 0. remainder=1%2=1, remainders=[1,0,0,1], n=1//2=0. Step 5: n=0, stop. Return [1,0,0,1] (binary 1001 when reversed).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Understanding Division by 2 for Binary Extraction
ladders.desc: Grasp why repeatedly dividing by 2 yields binary digits from LSB to MSB.
ladders.explanation: This core logic explains the mathematical foundation. Intuition: Any decimal number n can be expressed in binary as bₖbₖ₋₁...b₁b₀, where each bᵢ is 0 or 1, and n = bₖ×2ᵏ + bₖ₋₁×2ᵏ⁻¹ + ... + b₁×2¹ + b₀×2⁰. Dividing n by 2: n // 2 = bₖ×2ᵏ⁻¹ + ... + b₁×2⁰, and remainder n % 2 = b₀. Thus, each division peels off the least significant bit (b₀) and reduces the problem to the remaining higher bits. Algorithm insight: While n > 0, record n % 2, then set n = n // 2 to process next bit. Time: O(log n) as number of bits. Space: O(1) if just printing, but O(log n) if storing. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the while loop. Common mistakes: misunderstanding that remainders give bits in reverse order (LSB first), or not seeing why division by 2 works (it's because binary is base-2). Real code pattern: Conceptual, no full code—focus on why while n>0: bit = n%2; n//=2 yields bits.

examples.trace: 5 in binary: 101 = 1×2² + 0×2¹ + 1×2⁰. Step 1: n=5, 5%2=1 (b₀=1), 5//2=2 (remaining: 1×2¹ + 0×2⁰). Step 2: n=2, 2%2=0 (b₁=0), 2//2=1 (remaining: 1×2⁰). Step 3: n=1, 1%2=1 (b₂=1), 1//2=0. Stop. Bits collected: 1,0,1 (LSB first).

ladders.title: Building Block — Integer Division and Modulo Operations
ladders.desc: Master the use of // and % operators to extract quotient and remainder.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves performing integer division (//) and modulo (%) on integers. Intuition: For any integers a and b (b>0), a // b gives the quotient (how many times b fits into a, discarding remainder), and a % b gives the remainder (what's left after dividing). In binary conversion with b=2, these operations isolate bits: n % 2 gives the current binary digit (0 or 1), and n // 2 reduces the number for the next digit. Algorithm: For input n, compute quotient = n // 2 and remainder = n % 2. Time and space: O(1) per operation. Connection to L2: These operations implement the division-by-2 logic. Common mistakes: confusing / (float division) with // (integer division), or misunderstanding that % gives remainder (e.g., -1 % 2 = 1 in Python, but we assume non-negative n). Real code pattern: quotient = n // 2; remainder = n % 2.

examples.trace: 7 divided by 2: 2 fits into 7 three times (2×3=6), remainder is 7-6=1. So, 7 // 2 = 3, 7 % 2 = 1.
10 divided by 2: 2 fits into 10 five times (2×5=10), remainder is 10-10=0. So, 10 // 2 = 5, 10 % 2 = 0.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — While Loop for Repetition
ladders.desc: Use a while loop to repeat operations until a condition is met.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves implementing a while loop to perform actions repeatedly. Intuition: A while loop executes a block of code as long as a given condition is true. In binary conversion, we need to divide repeatedly until the number becomes 0. Algorithm: Initialize a variable; while condition (e.g., n > 0) is true, execute loop body (compute remainder, update n), then recheck condition. Time: depends on iterations; here O(log n). Space: O(1) for loop variables. Connection to L3: The loop uses division and modulo operations inside. Common mistakes: infinite loops if condition never becomes false (e.g., not updating n), or off-by-one errors. Real code pattern: while n > 0: # do something; n = n // 2.

examples.input: Print numbers from 3 down to 0 using while
examples.trace: Initialize n=3. While n >= 0: print n; n = n - 1. Step 1: n=3 >=0, print 3, n=2. Step 2: n=2 >=0, print 2, n=1. Step 3: n=1 >=0, print 1, n=0. Step 4: n=0 >=0, print 0, n=-1. Step 5: n=-1 not >=0, stop.

examples.input: Sum digits of 5 by repeated subtraction until 0
examples.trace: Goal: sum=0, n=5. While n > 0: sum += n; n -= 1. Step 1: n=5>0, sum=0+5=5, n=4. Step 2: n=4>0, sum=5+4=9, n=3. Step 3: n=3>0, sum=9+3=12, n=2. Step 4: n=2>0, sum=12+2=14, n=1. Step 5: n=1>0, sum=14+1=15, n=0. Step 6: n=0 not >0, stop. Sum=15 (but this example is illustrative; actual sum of digits 5 is 5).

testCases.input: While loop to count down from 5 to 1
While loop to add 2 starting at 0 until 6
While loop to multiply 3 by itself until >20
While loop to subtract 1 from 4 until 0
While loop to print even numbers from 0 to 6
While loop to sum numbers from 1 to 4
While loop to find first power of 2 >5

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Binary Number System
ladders.desc: Learn what binary numbers are and how they represent values using 0s and 1s.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers the binary numeral system (base-2). Intuition: Binary uses only two digits, 0 and 1, to represent numbers. Each digit is called a bit, and each position represents a power of 2, starting from 2⁰ at the rightmost (least significant bit). For example, binary 1011 means 1×2³ + 0×2² + 1×2¹ + 1×2⁰ = 8+0+2+1=11 in decimal. Key concepts: bits, positions (LSB to MSB), and conversion by summing powers of 2. Time and space: not applicable for conceptual understanding. Connection to L4: Binary knowledge is prerequisite for why we divide by 2. Common mistakes: confusing binary with decimal (e.g., thinking 10 in binary is ten), or misplacing bit positions. Real code pattern: Conceptual—no code, but understanding that e.g., bin(5) in Python gives '0b101'.

examples.trace: Binary 110: positions from right: bit0=0 (2⁰), bit1=1 (2¹), bit2=1 (2²). Calculate: 1×2² + 1×2¹ + 0×2⁰ = 1×4 + 1×2 + 0×1 = 4+2+0=6.

approaches.name: Built-in Function O(1)
approaches.summary: Use language-specific built-in functions like bin() in Python or Integer.toBinaryString() in Java to convert the decimal to binary instantly. This approach is optimal for practical coding where performance and simplicity are prioritized.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Convert Decimal to Binary Using Built-in Functions
ladders.desc: Complete solution using language-specific built-in functions to convert decimal to binary instantly.
ladders.explanation: This approach leverages the built-in binary conversion functions provided by programming languages to solve the decimal-to-binary conversion problem with optimal efficiency. The intuition is that modern programming languages have highly optimized, pre-implemented functions for common operations like base conversion, which abstract away the underlying mathematical complexity. By using these functions, we achieve O(1) time and space complexity, making this the most practical solution for real-world applications where performance and code simplicity are prioritized. The algorithm is straightforward: take an integer input, call the built-in binary conversion function (e.g., bin() in Python), and handle the output format to return a clean binary string. Step-by-step: 1. Accept an integer n as input. 2. Validate that n is non-negative (since built-in functions typically handle negative numbers differently, but for this problem, we assume non-negative input for clarity). 3. Call the built-in function (e.g., bin(n) in Python) to get a binary representation. 4. Process the output to remove any language-specific prefixes (e.g., '0b' in Python) and return the binary string. Pseudocode: function decimalToBinaryBuiltin(n): if n < 0: return 'Invalid input' binary_str = bin(n) return binary_str[2:]  # Remove '0b' prefix. Time complexity is O(1) because the built-in function executes in constant time, regardless of input size. Space complexity is O(1) as only a fixed amount of extra memory is used for the output string, which scales with the number of bits but is considered constant in big-O notation for fixed-size integers. This connects to the parent problem by providing a complete, efficient solution. Common mistakes include not handling negative inputs, forgetting to remove prefixes, or misinterpreting the output format. To avoid these, always check input constraints and test with edge cases. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: def decimal_to_binary_builtin(n): return bin(n)[2:] if n >= 0 else 'Invalid'.

examples.trace: Input: n = 5. Step 1: Validate n >= 0 (5 >= 0, valid). Step 2: Call bin(5), which returns '0b101'. Step 3: Remove prefix '0b' to get '101'. Output: '101'.
Input: n = 10. Step 1: Validate n >= 0 (10 >= 0, valid). Step 2: Call bin(10), which returns '0b1010'. Step 3: Remove prefix '0b' to get '1010'. Output: '1010'.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Calling Built-in Binary Conversion Function
ladders.desc: Implement the helper function that invokes the language-specific built-in binary conversion.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on the core action of using a built-in function to convert an integer to its binary representation. The intuition is that programming languages provide optimized, pre-written functions for common tasks, and understanding how to call and utilize these functions is crucial for efficient coding. This sub-problem involves taking an integer input, invoking the built-in binary conversion function (e.g., bin() in Python), and returning the raw output. The algorithm: 1. Accept an integer n. 2. Call the built-in function (e.g., bin(n)). 3. Return the result as is, without any post-processing. Pseudocode: function callBuiltinBinary(n): return bin(n). Time complexity is O(1) as the built-in function operates in constant time. Space complexity is O(1) for the function call overhead. This feeds into the level above (L0) by providing the binary string that will later be formatted. Common mistakes include passing non-integer inputs or misunderstanding the function's behavior for negative numbers. To avoid, ensure input is an integer and refer to language documentation. Real code pattern: def call_builtin_binary(n): return bin(n).

examples.trace: Input: n = 5. Step 1: Call bin(5). Output: '0b101'.
Input: n = 10. Step 1: Call bin(10). Output: '0b1010'.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Understanding Built-in Function Output Format
ladders.desc: Learn how built-in binary functions format their output, including prefixes and string representation.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the central insight that built-in binary conversion functions return a string with specific formatting, such as a prefix (e.g., '0b' in Python) to indicate binary representation. The intuition is that these functions are designed for clarity and consistency across languages, and understanding their output format is key to using them effectively. The technique involves examining the string returned by the function: for example, bin(n) in Python returns a string like '0b101' for n=5, where '0b' is a prefix and '101' is the binary digits. The algorithm: 1. Call the built-in function on a sample integer. 2. Analyze the returned string to identify any prefixes or special characters. 3. Understand that the binary digits start after the prefix. Pseudocode: function analyzeFormat(n): binary_str = bin(n); print('Prefix:', binary_str[:2], 'Digits:', binary_str[2:]). Time and space complexity are O(1) as it involves simple string operations. This connects to L1 by explaining why post-processing (removing prefixes) is necessary in the full solution. Common mistakes include assuming the output is a pure binary string or misidentifying the prefix length. To avoid, test with multiple inputs and read language specs. Real code pattern: def analyze_format(n): result = bin(n); return result[:2], result[2:].

examples.trace: Input: n = 5. Step 1: Call bin(5) → '0b101'. Step 2: Prefix is first 2 chars: '0b'. Step 3: Digits are from index 2 onward: '101'. Output: Prefix: 0b, Digits: 101.
Input: n = 10. Step 1: Call bin(10) → '0b1010'. Step 2: Prefix: '0b'. Step 3: Digits: '1010'. Output: Prefix: 0b, Digits: 1010.

ladders.title: Building Block — String Slicing to Remove Prefixes
ladders.desc: Use string slicing operations to extract the binary digits from the built-in function output.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves using string slicing to remove the prefix (e.g., '0b') from the output of a built-in binary conversion function. The intuition is that strings in programming languages can be manipulated by specifying indices to extract substrings, allowing us to clean up formatted outputs. The technique requires understanding how string indexing works: in Python, for example, a string '0b101' has indices 0 for '0', 1 for 'b', 2 for '1', etc. To get only the binary digits, we slice from index 2 to the end. The algorithm: 1. Start with a string s that has a known prefix (e.g., '0b'). 2. Determine the length of the prefix (e.g., 2 characters). 3. Use slicing to create a new string starting from the index after the prefix. Pseudocode: function removePrefix(s, prefix_length): return s[prefix_length:]. Time complexity is O(k) where k is the length of the string, but since prefix length is fixed and small, it's effectively O(1). Space complexity is O(1) for the new string. This connects to L2 by applying the knowledge of output format to clean the string. Common mistakes include incorrect index calculation or assuming fixed prefix length across languages. To avoid, verify prefix length and use dynamic methods if needed. Real code pattern: def remove_prefix(s): return s[2:]  # Assuming '0b' prefix.

examples.trace: Input: s = '0b101'. Step 1: Prefix length is 2 ('0b'). Step 2: Slice from index 2 to end: s[2:] → '101'. Output: '101'.
Input: s = '0b1010'. Step 1: Prefix length is 2. Step 2: Slice s[2:] → '1010'. Output: '1010'.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Calling a Built-in Function with an Argument
ladders.desc: Learn how to invoke a pre-defined function in a programming language by passing an input argument.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation covers the basics of calling a built-in function with an argument, which is essential for using language features like bin(). The intuition is that functions are reusable blocks of code that perform specific tasks, and calling them involves providing inputs (arguments) to get outputs. For example, in Python, bin() is a built-in function that takes an integer argument and returns a string. The algorithm: 1. Identify the function name (e.g., bin). 2. Prepare the argument (e.g., an integer n). 3. Call the function by writing its name followed by parentheses containing the argument. Pseudocode: function callFunction(argument): return builtinFunction(argument). Time and space complexity are O(1) as it's a simple function call. This connects to L3 by being the step before string slicing. Common mistakes include misspelling function names, passing wrong argument types, or forgetting parentheses. To avoid, practice with simple examples and refer to documentation. Real code pattern: result = bin(5)  # Calls bin with argument 5.

examples.trace: Input: argument = 5. Step 1: Identify function bin. Step 2: Call bin(5). Output: '0b101'.
Input: argument = 10. Step 1: Call bin(10). Output: '0b1010'.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Integers and Binary Representation
ladders.desc: Learn the basic concept that integers can be represented in different numeral systems, such as decimal and binary.
ladders.explanation: This simplest prerequisite concept introduces the idea that numbers can be expressed in various bases, with decimal (base-10) and binary (base-2) being common in computing. The intuition is that the same quantity can be written differently depending on the base: for example, the decimal number 5 equals the binary number 101. This foundation is crucial because built-in functions like bin() convert between these representations. The concept involves: decimal uses digits 0-9, while binary uses digits 0 and 1. For instance, decimal 5 means 5 units, and binary 101 means 1*2^2 + 0*2^1 + 1*2^0 = 4 + 0 + 1 = 5. There's no algorithm here, just understanding. Time and space complexity are not applicable as it's conceptual. This connects to L4 by explaining why we convert integers to binary. Common mistakes include confusing binary with other bases or misinterpreting digit places. To avoid, practice converting small numbers manually. Real code pattern: Not applicable, but in learning: decimal 10 = binary 1010.

examples.trace: Input: Decimal number 5. Concept: Binary uses base-2. Calculation: 5 in decimal = 1*4 + 0*2 + 1*1 = 101 in binary. Output: Binary representation is 101.
Input: Decimal 10. Calculation: 10 = 1*8 + 0*4 + 1*2 + 0*1 = 1010 in binary. Output: Binary 1010.

approaches.name: Bit Manipulation O(log n)
approaches.summary: Extract bits from the decimal number using bitwise operations like right shifts and AND with 1, constructing the binary string from least to most significant bit. This method is efficient and teaches low-level computer arithmetic concepts.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Convert Decimal to Binary Using Bit Manipulation
ladders.desc: Complete implementation converting decimal to binary string using right shifts and bitwise AND.
ladders.explanation: This solution converts a non-negative decimal integer to its binary string representation using bit manipulation. The intuition is that binary representation is essentially expressing a number as a sum of powers of 2, where each bit position corresponds to a specific power. By repeatedly extracting the least significant bit (LSB) using n & 1, we can build the binary string from right to left. The algorithm works by: 1) Handling the special case of 0, 2) While n > 0, extract LSB using n & 1, append to result, right shift n by 1, 3) Reverse the result since we built it from LSB to MSB. Time complexity is O(log n) as we process each bit once. Space complexity is O(log n) for storing the binary string. This connects to L1 by using the bit extraction subroutine. Common mistakes include: forgetting to handle n=0 case, not reversing the result, using string concatenation inefficiently (O(n²) time), and not considering negative numbers (though problem specifies non-negative). Real pattern: while n > 0: bits.append(str(n & 1)); n >>= 1; return '0' if not bits else ''.join(reversed(bits)).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Extract and Store Bits Using Right Shift
ladders.desc: Implement the core loop that extracts bits from a number and builds a list of bits.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine performs the essential bit extraction process. The intuition is that repeatedly applying n & 1 gives us the current LSB, and n >>= 1 removes that bit, moving to the next one. This is like peeling layers of an onion from the rightmost side. The algorithm: 1) Initialize empty list bits, 2) While n > 0: append str(n & 1) to bits, right shift n by 1, 3) Return bits list (which will be in reverse order). Time: O(log n) for the loop. Space: O(log n) for bits list. This feeds into L0 by providing the extracted bits that need reversal. Common mistakes: infinite loop if forgetting to update n, appending integer instead of string, incorrect order understanding. Real pattern: bits = []; while n > 0: bits.append(str(n & 1)); n >>= 1; return bits.

examples.trace:
n=13
Iteration 1: n&1=1 → bits=['1'], n=13>>1=6
Iteration 2: n&1=0 → bits=['1','0'], n=6>>1=3
Iteration 3: n&1=1 → bits=['1','0','1'], n=3>>1=1
Iteration 4: n&1=1 → bits=['1','0','1','1'], n=1>>1=0
Loop ends, return ['1','0','1','1'] (note: this is LSB-first, so actual bits are reversed)

n=6
Iteration 1: n&1=0 → bits=['0'], n=6>>1=3
Iteration 2: n&1=1 → bits=['0','1'], n=3>>1=1
Iteration 3: n&1=1 → bits=['0','1','1'], n=1>>1=0
Loop ends, return ['0','1','1'] (LSB-first: 0,1,1 corresponds to binary 110)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Extract Least Significant Bit Using AND with 1
ladders.desc: Understand and implement extracting the rightmost bit of a number.
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique: using bitwise AND with 1 (n & 1) to get the LSB. Intuition: In binary, any number's LSB is 1 if odd, 0 if even. The AND operation with 1 masks all bits except the LSB because 1 in binary is ...0001. So n & 1 isolates just the last bit. For example, 5 (101) & 1 (001) = 1 (001). The algorithm is simple: return n & 1. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). This feeds into L1 as the operation inside the loop. Common mistakes: confusing & with &&, not understanding bitwise vs logical. Real pattern: lsb = n & 1.

examples.trace:
5 in binary: 101
1 in binary: 001
AND operation:
  101
& 001
  ---
  001 → decimal 1

12 in binary: 1100
1 in binary: 0001
AND operation:
  1100
& 0001
  ----
  0000 → decimal 0

ladders.title: Building Block — Right Shift Operation to Remove LSB
ladders.desc: Implement and understand the right shift operation (>>) on integers.
ladders.explanation: The right shift operation n >>= 1 divides n by 2 and truncates, effectively removing the LSB. Intuition: Shifting bits right by 1 position moves all bits one place to the right, discarding the LSB and filling left with 0. For example, 13 (1101) >> 1 = 6 (110). This is equivalent to integer division by 2. The algorithm: n = n >> 1 or n >>= 1. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). This feeds into L1 by advancing to the next bit. Common mistakes: confusing >> with <<, not understanding it's integer division. Real pattern: n >>= 1.

examples.trace:
13 in binary: 1101
Right shift by 1: move all bits right
  1101 → 0110 (binary)
0110 in decimal = 6

10 in binary: 1010
Right shift by 1: move all bits right
  1010 → 0101 (binary)
0101 in decimal = 5

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Bitwise AND Between Two Numbers
ladders.desc: Implement and understand the bitwise AND operation (&).
ladders.explanation: Bitwise AND compares corresponding bits of two numbers: output bit is 1 only if both input bits are 1. Intuition: It's like a filter that keeps only bits where both numbers have 1s. For example, 5 & 3: 101 & 011 = 001. The algorithm: result = a & b. Time: O(1) for fixed-size integers. Space: O(1). This feeds into L2 as the mechanism to extract LSB. Common mistakes: confusing with logical AND, not understanding bit-level operation. Real pattern: result = a & b.

examples.trace:
5 in binary: 101
3 in binary: 011
Bitwise AND:
  101
& 011
  ---
  001 → decimal 1

12 in binary: 1100
7 in binary: 0111
Bitwise AND:
  1100
& 0111
  ----
  0100 → decimal 4

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Number System Basics
ladders.desc: Understand binary representation and place values.
ladders.explanation: Binary is base-2 number system using only digits 0 and 1. Each position represents a power of 2, starting from 2^0 at rightmost. Intuition: Just like decimal has ones, tens, hundreds (powers of 10), binary has ones, twos, fours, eights (powers of 2). For example, 1011 in binary = 1×2³ + 0×2² + 1×2¹ + 1×2⁰ = 8+0+2+1=11 in decimal. The algorithm to convert binary string to decimal: sum(digit × 2^position) from right to left. Time: O(length of binary string). Space: O(1). This feeds into L4 by providing context for bit operations. Common mistakes: confusing binary with decimal, incorrect place values. Real pattern: decimal = sum(int(bit) * (2**pos) for pos, bit in enumerate(reversed(binary_str))).

examples.trace:
Binary: 1 0 1
Positions from right: position 0: 1, position 1: 0, position 2: 1
Calculation: (1×2²) + (0×2¹) + (1×2⁰) = 4 + 0 + 1 = 5

Binary: 1 1 0 0
Positions: pos0:0, pos1:0, pos2:1, pos3:1
Calculation: (1×2³) + (1×2²) + (0×2¹) + (0×2⁰) = 8 + 4 + 0 + 0 = 12
